notable.notable_blurb: Director of the U.S. Mint; Pennsylvania House Speaker

burial.plot_notes: Laurel Hill East Cemetery, Philadelphia PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Wikipedia.

narrative_blocks.header: He built the U.S. Mint's famous coin cabinet
narrative_blocks.body: As Director of the United States Mint from 1853 to 1861, Snowden enlarged the Mint's collection by striking restrikes of rare older coins for collectors, and became a noted numismatist of his day.

narrative_blocks.header: Twice Speaker of the Pennsylvania House
narrative_blocks.body: Admitted to the Venango County bar at nineteen, Snowden represented his district in the Pennsylvania House and was elected its Speaker in 1842 and again in 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote books on coins and Bible money
narrative_blocks.body: A prolific author on numismatics, Snowden published works on the Mint's coin cabinet, the medals of Washington, and "The Coins of the Bible."

narrative_blocks.header: State treasurer who steadied Pennsylvania's finances
narrative_blocks.body: As Pennsylvania State Treasurer in 1845-46, Snowden instituted reforms that placed the state treasury on a sound financial footing.

parents.mother_research_notes: Daughter of Sarah Ann Engle Patterson.

burial.plot_notes: Laurel Hill East Cemetery, Philadelphia PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: She married into the Mint and militia
narrative_blocks.body: Susan Engle Patterson, daughter of Maj. Gen. Robert Patterson, married James Ross Snowden in 1848 at Market Square Presbyterian in Harrisburg and raised their large family in Philadelphia.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated from the College of New Jersey (Princeton) in 1787; later called the oldest living graduate.

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor of the founding Harrisburg congregation
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1793, Snowden served the united churches of Harrisburg, Paxtang, and Derry, becoming first pastor of the English-speaking congregation now known as Market Square Presbyterian, and taught classics to support his salary.

narrative_blocks.header: He recorded Washington praying at Valley Forge
narrative_blocks.body: In his diary Snowden set down, from the Quaker Isaac Potts himself, the famous account of General Washington kneeling alone in prayer in the woods at Valley Forge during the war's darkest winter.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a Wyoming Massacre survivor
narrative_blocks.body: Snowden married Sarah Gustine, who as an infant had escaped the 1778 Wyoming Massacre down the Susquehanna and was said to be its last living survivor.

narrative_blocks.header: She was called the last survivor of the Wyoming Massacre
narrative_blocks.body: As a three-year-old, Sarah Gustine escaped the 1778 Wyoming Massacre with her father down the Susquehanna; at her death in 1856 she was said to be the last living survivor of that day.

notable.notable_blurb: Major general in the Mexican-American and Civil Wars

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow general in Scott's 1847 march on Mexico City
served the Confederacy on Gen. Bee’s Manassas staff

burial.plot_notes: Laurel Hill East Cemetery, Philadelphia PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Wikipedia.

narrative_blocks.body: A major general in the Mexican-American War, Patterson served as Winfield Scott's second-in-command, fighting at Veracruz and Cerro Gordo on the march toward Mexico City.

narrative_blocks.header: Blamed for the Union rout at Bull Run
narrative_blocks.body: Recalled as a major general in 1861, Patterson failed to pin down Confederate general Joseph Johnston in the Shenandoah; Johnston slipped away to reinforce Bull Run, and Patterson was widely blamed for the Union's first major defeat.

narrative_blocks.header: An immigrant's son who built a cotton empire
narrative_blocks.body: Patterson came to America as a boy after his father was banished for joining the 1798 Irish Rebellion; he grew wealthy in Philadelphia manufacturing, eventually owning some thirty cotton mills.

narrative_blocks.header: His Louisiana sugar plantation rested on enslaved labor
narrative_blocks.body: Among Patterson's many enterprises was a sugar plantation in Louisiana — an operation, like all antebellum Louisiana sugar estates, worked by enslaved people.

narrative_blocks.header: Grant and Sherman bore his coffin
narrative_blocks.body: When Patterson died at eighty-nine in 1881, his pallbearers included Ulysses S. Grant and William Tecumseh Sherman; his sons Francis and Robert had both served as Union generals.
